# Jobs-to-be-Done

Customer-research lens popularised by Clayton Christensen. Customers do not
buy products — they hire products to make progress on a "job" they are
trying to get done in a specific situation. A job has functional, emotional,
and social dimensions, and the unit of analysis is the situation, not the
demographic.

Implications for product work:
- Segmentation by job, not by persona — different people in the same
  situation hire the same product.
- Competition is defined by the job, so a spreadsheet can compete with a
  hiring agency.
- Switching is observable: study moments of "first hire" and "fire" of
  the prior solution.

When to reach for it: discovery work where the brief asks "build feature X"
without explaining what the user is trying to accomplish, or when the team
keeps shipping into a saturated category and demand stays flat.
